title: The Weaver's Boy
subtitle: Apprentice Weavers of Oxyrhynchus, 54--183 CE

sections.body_html:
In the thirteenth year of Nero, on the twenty-fifth of Thoth – 18 September 66 CE – a weaver named Tryphon walked his son to someone else’s loom.

The boy was called Thoonis. He was not yet of age – oudepo onta ton eton – perhaps twelve, perhaps fourteen, the papyrus does not say.1 His mother was Saraeus, daughter of Apion, who had brought to her marriage with Tryphon a dowry of 40 silver drachmas in cash, a pair of gold earrings worth 20 drachmas, and a milk-white robe valued at 12.2 His father was a weaver, the son of a weaver, the grandson of a weaver. Three generations at the loom, at least. Probably more.

The master was Ptolemaeus, son of Pausirion, himself the son of Pausiris – a weaver from a weaving family, living in the same city, working the same trade, known to Tryphon through the dense network of obligations and acquaintances that bound the weaving quarter together.3 Ptolemaeus would teach Thoonis gerdiaken technen – the weaver’s art – “in all its branches of which Ptolemaeus has knowledge.”4

The contract was written. The terms were specific. The duration was one year.

And that is how a boy became a weaver in Roman Oxyrhynchus: not by watching his father, not by playing under the loom as a toddler and gradually absorbing the rhythm of the shuttle, but by legal instrument, with penalty clauses and daily-absence fines and a mutual obligation enforceable by the state.

sections.title: I. The Weaver at the Serapeum
sections.body_html:
To understand why Tryphon gave his son to another man’s workshop, you have to understand who Tryphon was. And we can, because he left behind an archive.

Not deliberately. Nobody in Roman Egypt kept their documents as a gift to posterity. They kept them because the bureaucracy demanded it: contracts, receipts, tax records, petitions, census declarations. The state ran on papyrus, and the citizens of its cities filed everything. What we call the Tryphon archive is a collection of documents spanning roughly thirty years of one weaver’s life, recovered from the rubbish heaps of Oxyrhynchus and now scattered across the collections of the Egypt Exploration Fund and the libraries that house them.5

Tryphon, son of Dionysius, son of Tryphon. A “Persian of the Epigone” – a legal classification, not an ethnicity, a vestigial label from the Ptolemaic military settlement system that by Tryphon’s day meant nothing except a tax category.6 In 55 CE, when he bought half a three-storied house near the Serapeum, the notary described him in the precise physical detail that Egyptian contracts demanded: about 47 years old, medium height, fair complexion, long face, a slight squint, and a scar on his right wrist.7

The squint matters. Three years earlier, Tryphon had traveled over 200 kilometers to Alexandria for a medical examination before the prefect of Egypt himself – Gnaeus Vergilius Capito, praefect of Upper and Lower Egypt. The result: a military exemption certificate. “Release from service was granted… to Tryphon, son of Dionysius, weaver, suffering from cataract and shortness of sight.”8

Gerdios – weaver. The word appears in the exemption like a job title on a medical form. It was his identity, legally and economically. And his eyes were failing. A weaver with cataracts. A man whose craft demanded close focus on fine threads, working at a loom day after day, squinting at the warp and weft – and his sight was going. He was forty-four when they examined him.

He kept working for at least another fourteen years.

sections.title: II. The Loom
sections.body_html:
Two years after the medical exemption, in August 54 CE, Tryphon bought his tools.

The transaction was conducted through the bank of Sarapion, son of Lochus, near the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus – the great temple complex that dominated the neighborhood where Tryphon lived and worked.9 The seller was a man named Ammonius, son of Ammonius, who was illiterate; a scribe named Heraclides, son of Dionysius, wrote for him.10

What Tryphon purchased was a histos gerdiakos – a professional weaver’s loom. It measured three weavers’ cubits minus two palms – roughly 1.35 meters, about four and a half feet wide – and came equipped with two rollers (antia), two beams (histopodes), and one epimitron, a supplementary beam or measuring device whose exact function scholars still debate.11 The price was 20 silver drachmas.

Twenty drachmas for a loom. To put that in proportion: a month later, Tryphon would buy half a three-storied house for 32 talents of copper.12 A military cloak was worth about 30 drachmas. An unskilled laborer earned roughly one drachma per day.13 The loom was not cheap, but it was not extravagant either. It was a working tool, built to a standard size, sold with a guarantee and a penalty clause: if the sale was challenged, Ammonius would refund the price plus half again, and pay damages besides.14

This was the machine on which Thoonis would learn his trade. Not exactly this loom, perhaps – he was apprenticed to Ptolemaeus, not to his father – but one like it. The upright warp-weighted loom of Roman Egypt, on which fabric was woven from top to bottom, the weaver standing or sitting as the work progressed, the shuttle passing through the shed created by the heddle, the beater pressing each weft thread into place. The sound of it – the rhythmic thud of the beater, the clatter of the shuttle, the creak of the frame – would have been the dominant sound of the weaving quarter, audible from the street, marking the hours like a slow clock.

sections.title: III. The Marriage
sections.body_html:
Tryphon’s marriage to Saraeus, Thoonis’s mother, took place on the twenty-second of May, 37 CE – the first year of Gaius Caesar, whom history would call Caligula.15 Tryphon was about twenty-nine. We do not know Saraeus’s age. She came under the guardianship of a man named Onnophris, son of Antipater.

The dowry was 72 drachmas total: 40 in cash, deposited through the bank of Sarapion son of Kleandrus near the Serapeum; gold earrings valued at 20 drachmas; and a milk-white robe worth 12.16 This was a modest dowry by elite standards, but respectable for a craftsman’s household. The earrings were real gold. The robe was specified as leukos galaktinos – milk-white, a term that suggests quality fabric, possibly fine linen.17

The contract included divorce provisions. If they separated, Saraeus would keep the earrings at their current value. Tryphon committed to repaying the full 72 drachmas by the thirtieth of Phaophi in the second year of Gaius Caesar – about five months later – or face a fifty percent surcharge.18 The fact that he agreed to such a short repayment window suggests either confidence in his income or pressure from Saraeus’s family.

Tryphon could not write. A scribe named Leon signed for him. Onnophris, Saraeus’s guardian, also required a scribe – Theon, son of Paaeis. And Saraeus herself? Her acknowledgment was written “in astonishingly badly spelled Greek” by one Didymus, son of Boethus.19 Three scribes for one marriage contract. Literacy in Roman Oxyrhynchus was a service you purchased, and the weaving quarter purchased it often.

This was Tryphon’s second marriage. He had previously been married to a woman named Demetrous, whose name appears in earlier documents from the archive.20 Saraeus became the mother of Thoonis – the boy who, nearly thirty years later, would be handed over to the loom of Ptolemaeus.

sections.title: IV. Why Not His Own Father?
sections.body_html:
The question that nags at every reading of P.Oxy. 2.275 is this: why did Tryphon apprentice his son to someone else?

He was a weaver. He owned a loom. He had decades of experience. The trade was hereditary – Tryphon’s father Dionysius was a weaver, and his grandfather Tryphon before him.21 The most natural thing in the world would have been for Thoonis to learn at his father’s side.

And yet the contract exists. Tryphon paid – or rather, was paid – to have his son trained by another master. The financial terms are unusual: Tryphon was responsible for feeding and clothing the boy at his own expense, but received 5 drachmas per month from Ptolemaeus for food costs, plus 12 drachmas at the end of the year for clothing.22 This is not the arrangement of a father unloading a burden. It is the arrangement of a father investing in a specific outcome.

We can guess at the reasons. Perhaps Tryphon’s failing eyesight – cataracts and “shortness of sight,” documented fourteen years earlier – had worsened to the point where he could no longer demonstrate fine techniques.23 Perhaps the weaving community of Oxyrhynchus maintained an informal standard that sons should train under other masters, to broaden their skills, to learn different specializations, to prevent the degradation that comes from an isolated workshop.24 The scholar who reconstructed the weaving families of Oxyrhynchus showed that Pausiris – Ptolemaeus’s father – apprenticed at least three of his own sons to other weavers, not to himself.25 It was a pattern. Deliberate. A community strategy for maintaining quality across generations.

Or perhaps it was simpler than that. Perhaps Tryphon, squinting at the loom with worsening eyes and a scar on his right wrist, knew that his son needed a teacher who could still see the threads.

sections.title: V. The Terms
sections.body_html:
The contract deserves to be read closely.

Thoonis was to serve Ptolemaeus for one year. One year was short – most weaving apprenticeships in Roman Egypt lasted two to five years, with an average of about two and a half.26 The brevity may suggest that Thoonis had already received some informal training from his father and needed only formal finishing, or it may reflect the particular arrangement between two families who knew each other well.

Tryphon fed and clothed the boy. Ptolemaeus paid 5 drachmas per month toward food and 12 drachmas at the end for clothing.27 The direction of payment is notable – in most apprenticeships, the master received money from the family. Here the master paid the family. This reversal suggests that Thoonis was already productive enough to contribute to Ptolemaeus’s workshop, or that the arrangement was less a true apprenticeship than a placement within the professional network.

The attendance clause was precise. If the boy missed any days, Tryphon was required to produce him for an equivalent number of days after the contract period expired. If he failed, he forfeited one drachma per day – a full laborer’s daily wage.28 The penalty for withdrawing the boy before the year was up was 100 drachmas, plus an equal sum to the treasury.29 One hundred drachmas was roughly twenty months of the food payments. It was serious money. It was meant to be.

And the obligation ran both ways. “If Ptolemaeus fails to instruct the boy thoroughly,” the contract reads, “he is to be liable to the same penalties.”30 Kathos auton epistato – “as thoroughly as he knows it himself.” The master’s competence was the benchmark. Ptolemaeus was required to teach everything he knew, and if he withheld knowledge or cut corners, he owed 100 drachmas to Tryphon and 100 to the state.

Ptolemaeus signed the contract himself. Tryphon’s signature was written for him by someone else – consistent with every other document in the archive where Tryphon needed a scribe.31

sections.title: VI. The Quarter
sections.body_html:
Where did this happen? The documents, taken together, sketch a map.

Tryphon’s house was near the Serapeum – the great temple of Serapis that dominated the southern part of Oxyrhynchus. He bought his half-house on a street called Temgenouthis, “to the west of the lane leading to Shepherds’ Street.”32 The boundaries were described with the notarial precision that Egyptian contracts demanded: public roads to the south and east, the house of Tryphon’s mother Thamounis to the north, the house of one Tausiris (the seller’s sister) to the west, separated by a blind alley.

This was not an isolated address. Temgenouthis Square was a center of the weaving trade. When a slave weaver named Apollophanes died in 61 CE, his owner Sarapion filed a death notice specifying that the slave had been “registered in Temgenouthis Square.”33 The death notice was addressed to Philiscus, “farmer of the tax on weaving” – the man who held the contract to collect the occupational tax from the weavers of that district.34 Weavers, tax collectors, slave-weavers, all clustered in the same neighborhood, within earshot of each other’s looms.

But weavers were not confined to Temgenouthis. When a weaver named Sarapion filed a complaint about tax extortion around 50 CE, he identified himself as living “in Gymnasium Square quarter.”35 Another weaver, Alexandrus, lived “in the quarter of the square of Thoeris.”36 A third, Dius son of Peteuris, identified himself as being from “the weavers of the quarter of Poimenike” – the Shepherds’ Quarter, the same street whose lane ran past Tryphon’s house.37

Weaving was everywhere. It was not a single industrial district but a distributed trade, threaded through the city’s neighborhoods the way the warp threads through a loom. The sound of the shuttle was the background noise of Oxyrhynchus.

sections.title: VII. The Tax
sections.body_html:
Every weaver paid the gerdiake techne – the weaver’s trade tax. It was collected bimonthly by tax-farmers, private contractors who bid for the right to collect and kept a percentage for themselves. The earliest surviving receipt dates to 22-21 BCE, when an agent named Dachis collected 40 drachmas from a linen-weaver named Patemnophis.38

The tax rates varied. In 17 BCE, a linen-weaver named Petechonsis was assessed 20 drachmas, while a general weaver named Leukis paid only 2.39 The tenfold difference is startling. Perhaps linen-weavers – linyphoi, who worked with the more expensive flax fiber – were taxed at a higher rate than wool-weavers. Perhaps the difference reflected production volume, or workshop size, or some other criterion the surviving documents do not explain.40

By the mid-first century CE, the tax was collected in installments. A receipt from 49 CE records that Petemenophis son of Thoteus paid “four drachmas, total 4 drachmas, and the surcharges of 1 obol 1/2 obol” for the linen-weavers’ tax of year 9.41 A century later, in 167 CE, the rate had risen: a weaver named Porieuths paid “soiled drachmai eight, total 8 drachmai” for two months of the tax.42 The word ryparai – “soiled” – referred to coins of reduced silver content, a linguistic marker of the monetary inflation that was slowly debasing the currency throughout the second century.

The tax receipts pile up across the decades. Horos son of Phaeris in 90 CE.43 Pachnoumis son of Pakoibios in 191 CE.44 Besarion and his brothers in 197 CE – suggesting a family weaving operation paying as a unit.45 A woman, whose name is partly preserved as “Ch[…]os daughter of Senpetemenophis,” paid the weaver’s tax in 141 CE – evidence that women worked at the loom and were taxed for it.46 A slave-woman paid in 188 CE.47 The tax was universal. If you wove, you paid.

And sometimes the collection system broke. A document from the late second century records weavers who had fled – their names listed under the heading of those “not traced,” people who had disappeared to “unknown places” rather than face the cloth-weavers’ tax.48 The phenomenon had a name: anachoresis, flight. When the burden became unbearable, people simply left.

sections.title: VIII. The Extortionists
sections.body_html:
Three petitions from mid-first-century Oxyrhynchus – all filed with the same strategus, Tiberius Claudius Pasion – tell a story that the tax receipts alone cannot.

The first is from Sarapion, son of Theon, a weaver living in Gymnasium Square. “Apollophanes, ex-collector of the trade tax upon weavers,” he writes, “in the first year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar… using great violence seized from me a linen tunic which I was wearing, worth 8 drachmae.”49 The word is bia – violence. Apollophanes ripped the tunic off Sarapion’s body. Then he extorted 4 drachmas immediately and 2 drachmas each month for six months. Total take: 24 drachmas plus the tunic. Nearly a year’s legitimate tax, stolen piecemeal by a man who was no longer even the official collector.50

The second petition is from Alexandrus, son of Apollonius, a weaver in the Square of Thoeris quarter. The same Apollophanes “extorted from me among other people 16 drachmae of silver.”51 The phrase “among other people” is devastating in its casualness. Alexandrus was not a special target. He was one of many.

The third is from Dius, son of Peteuris, a weaver in the Shepherds’ Quarter. A different ex-collector, Ammonius, had extorted 40 drachmas in year 6 and another 40 in year 7 – “bit by bit,” kata mikron.52 Eighty drachmas total. The extortion was so severe that Dius fell into arrears on his legitimate trade tax. The corrupt collector had bled him dry, and now the state wanted its share too.

Three weavers, two quarters, two extortionists, one strategus. The petitions reveal a system vulnerable at every joint. The tax-farmers who held the collection contracts had enormous power over the tradespeople they assessed, and even when they lost their official positions, they apparently retained enough muscle to continue extracting payments. The weavers, working alone or in small workshops, had no collective defense – only the petition system, the slow machinery of appeal to an official who might or might not act.

We do not know what happened after the petitions were filed. The papyri preserve the complaints. They do not preserve the outcomes.

sections.title: IX. The Longer Apprenticeship
sections.body_html:
One hundred and seventeen years after Tryphon handed Thoonis to Ptolemaeus, another boy was bound to another loom.

The contract is P.Oxy. 4.725, dated the twenty-third of September, 183 CE, in the twenty-fourth year of the emperor Commodus. It is the most detailed weaving apprenticeship document to survive from the ancient world.53

The boy was named Thonis – the same name, almost, as Tryphon’s son Thoonis; common enough in Oxyrhynchus to be coincidence, though the echo is striking. He was a minor, apprenticed not by his father but by his uncle Ischyrion, son of Heradion.54 The father was dead. In this world, when a father died, a brother or uncle stepped into the gap. The family did not let the boy drift.

The master was Heraclas, son of Sarapion, also called Leon – a weaver, explicitly identified as gerdios.55 Like Ptolemaeus before him, Heraclas was illiterate. His subscription at the bottom of the contract was written by a third party: “I, Thonis also called Morous, son of Harthonis, wrote for him as he was illiterate.”56 Two illiterate weavers, a century apart, each signing apprenticeship contracts through someone else’s hand. The pattern was not exceptional. It was the trade.

But where Tryphon’s contract was brief and idiosyncratic, this one was comprehensive. Five years. From sunrise to sunset, every day, performing “all the orders that may be given to him by the said teacher on the same terms as the other apprentices.”57 On the same terms as the other apprentices – the phrase implies that Heraclas had multiple students, a workshop school of sorts, where boys worked side by side learning the same craft.

sections.title: X. The Graduated Scale
sections.body_html:
The wage structure of P.Oxy. 4.725 is an education in miniature economics.

For the first two years and seven months of the third year, Heraclas paid nothing for the boy’s labor.58 Pure training. The apprentice was a cost, not a revenue source: he ate food his uncle provided, he broke threads, he made mistakes, he slowed the master down. Then, in the remaining five months of year three, wages began: 12 drachmas per month. In year four: 16 drachmas. In year five: 24 drachmas.59

The curve is the boy’s value, expressed in silver. As he learned, he produced. As he produced, he earned. By the fifth year, at 24 drachmas per month, he was earning a living wage – more than many unskilled laborers made in the same period.

Alongside the wages, Heraclas provided one tunic per year of increasing value: 16 drachmas in the first year, then 20, 24, 28, and 32.60 The rising cost reflected two things: the boy was growing, and cloth was getting more expensive. Inflation in Roman Egypt was steady and relentless through the second century. The tunic that cost 16 drachmas in 183 CE would cost 32 five years later, partly because the boy needed more fabric and partly because the drachma bought less.

The boy received twenty holiday days per year for religious festivals – a substantial concession in a calendar that otherwise demanded work from dawn to dusk.61 Any absence beyond those twenty days, whether from “idleness or ill-health or disobedience or any other reason,” had to be made up after the contract period, without wages.62 The uncle was responsible for producing the boy on those make-up days. The master was responsible for feeding him during the regular period.

And at the end, Heraclas was required to teach the apprentice “as thoroughly as he knows it himself.”63 The same phrase as in Tryphon’s contract a century earlier. The same standard. The master’s full knowledge, transferred in full, or face 100 drachmas in penalties plus an equal amount to the treasury.

sections.title: XI. The Dead Slave and the Living Trade
sections.body_html:
Between the two apprenticeship contracts – between 66 and 183 CE – the weaving trade of Oxyrhynchus continued to produce its paper trail. And in that trail, a document from 61 CE stands out for what it reveals about the human beings behind the looms.

“To Philiscus, farmer of the tax on weaving, from Sarapion, son of Sarapion. My slave Apollophanes a weaver, registered in Temgenouthis Square, died during absence in the present 7th year of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator.”64

A slave who was a professional weaver. Registered by name, in a specific quarter, on the tax rolls. Working the same trade, at the same looms, in the same neighborhood as free weavers like Tryphon and Ptolemaeus. When Apollophanes died, his owner was required to notify the tax collector so the rolls could be updated – because the state tracked individual weavers by name and location, and a dead weaver meant lost revenue.65

The death notice specifies that Apollophanes died “during absence” – he was away from Oxyrhynchus when he died, somewhere the notice does not say. We do not know where he went, or why, or whether his absence was authorized or was itself a kind of flight. We know only that his owner filed the paperwork and that the tax farmer acknowledged it.

Slave and free weavers worked in the same trade, under the same tax regime, in the same quarters of the same city. Scholarship on Roman Egyptian apprenticeships has found that of 39 documented apprentices, 11 were enslaved – working “side by side under similar conditions” with free children.66 The loom did not distinguish between them. The tax collector did not distinguish between them. The skill was the same. The product was the same. The difference was that one went home to his own bed and the other went home to his master’s house, and that when one died away from home, someone filed a notice to save money on next year’s tax bill.

sections.title: XII. The Senate and the Weavers
sections.body_html:
Two centuries after Tryphon, the world had changed.

In 271-272 CE, the city senate of Oxyrhynchus – the boule, established by Septimius Severus at the turn of the third century – met to debate textile production. The minutes survive. They are the only record we have of the Oxyrhynchus senate discussing weaving, and they reveal a trade that had been transformed from a network of independent workshops into something closer to a managed industry.67

The problem was temple linen. The city was responsible for supplying woven cloth to the temples, and the supply chain was failing. Yarn-merchants – stemonopoioi – demanded 49 denarii for weaving-yarn and had received only 11 from the treasury.68 The senators negotiated: “Let the yarn-merchants be content with 19 denarii besides what was paid to them from the Treasury’s account.”69

Then the cloth-weavers themselves petitioned. They wanted more money. Prices had risen, they said, and so had the wages of their workmen – tois ergois ton ergatomenon, the workers who labored for them.70 The weavers had workmen. They were no longer solitary artisans at single looms. They were employers, running operations large enough to require hired labor, petitioning the senate collectively for better rates. The prytanis, the presiding officer, agreed: “Let more drachmae be given to the cloth-weavers, making 200 drachmae in all, on account of the rise in value of the materials.”71

The senate also required quality control. Samples had to be provided. Cloth-weavers appointed someone to test the temple linen.72 The state was not merely buying cloth; it was inspecting it, regulating it, negotiating prices in open session with the people who made it. The autonomous craftsman of Tryphon’s day – buying his own loom, training his own son, paying his own taxes – had become a contractor to the municipal government, bound by senatorial resolutions and quality inspections.

And by the end of the third century, even that arrangement would give way to something worse. An oath survives from the reign of Diocletian: “I acknowledge, swearing by the fortune of our lords Diocletian and Maximian, Augusti, that I will go up to Panopolis and serve in the weaving-works that serves the well-being of the most valiant soldiers.”73 Government weaving factories – gynaecea – where workers were compelled to serve and required to provide sureties guaranteeing they would not flee.74 The oath-taker was not a craftsman choosing his trade. He was a conscript.

From Tryphon’s loom to the factories of Panopolis. From a father buying tools at the bank near the Serapeum to a state that compelled labor and demanded oaths of submission. Two hundred and fifty years, and the weaver’s world had narrowed to a cell.

sections.title: XIII. What the Boy Learned
sections.body_html:
We do not have a curriculum for the weaver’s apprenticeship. No syllabus survives. But the contracts, the tax records, the household accounts, and the letters allow us to reconstruct what Thoonis would have learned in his year with Ptolemaeus, and what Thonis would have learned in his five years with Heraclas.

He learned to prepare the warp. The vertical threads, stretched taut on the loom’s two beams, had to be measured, cut, and attached with even tension – uneven tension produced uneven cloth, and uneven cloth was unsalable. A will from Oxyrhynchus, dated to the reign of Hadrian, bequeaths a “weaving rod” measuring five cubits – about 2.3 meters – as a named item alongside furniture, underscoring how valued such tools were.75

He learned the shuttle. The horizontal weft thread, carried back and forth through the shed created by lifting alternate warp threads, had to be thrown with consistent force and caught cleanly. The rhythm of the shuttle was the rhythm of the trade. A household expense account from the Augustan period records “the weaver’s breakfast, 1 obol; beer for the weaver, 1 obol” – the daily cost of keeping a working weaver fed as he threw the shuttle hour after hour, dawn to dusk.76

He learned to distinguish linen from wool, and perhaps to work both. Linen was the traditional Egyptian textile, woven from locally grown flax. Wool was increasingly important in the Roman period, as tastes shifted and military demand grew.77 A private letter from the second century CE captures the demand side: “I enclosed in the former packet a pattern of white-violet colour. I beg you to match it and buy me two drachmas’ weight, and send it to me at once… for the tunic is to be woven immediately.”78 The specificity of the color request – white-violet – and the urgency suggest a market where professional weavers were expected to match precise samples and work to tight deadlines.

He learned the business. Not just the craft but the economics: what to charge for a cloak (1 drachma 2 obols in the Augustan period), how to manage the bimonthly tax payments, how to deal with the tax-farmers who might arrive with violence or with a receipt.79 He learned that a weaver could own land – a register from the late second century records a linen-weaver named Theoninos with one and a half arouras, about an acre, while another weaver named Perseus owned three and a half.80 The trade could make you prosperous. Not rich, but stable.

And he learned, perhaps, that a dead weaver’s looms were worth fighting over. An inventory of a deceased weaver’s property from the early second century lists “a half share in three weavers’ looms which belonged to his father” – looms treated as inheritable property, listed alongside coffers, bronze boxes, and items that had been pawned for cash.81 The inventory bristles with pledges: a plate pawned for 20 drachmas, a box for 10, a flask for 4, a tunic for 8. The family was in difficulty. But the looms – the looms were named and counted. They were the foundation.

sections.title: XIV. The Women at the Loom
sections.body_html:
The apprenticeship contracts are about boys. Tryphon’s son. Ischyrion’s nephew. Boys brought to masters, bound by contracts, trained in a trade that the documents present as exclusively male.

It was not.

The tax receipts tell a different story. In 141 CE, a woman whose partially preserved name reads “Ch[…]os daughter of Senpetemenophis” paid the weaver’s tax.82 In 188 CE, a slave-woman paid it.83 Both were registered as weavers, both were taxed as weavers, and both presumably worked at looms in the same quarters where Tryphon and Ptolemaeus had worked a century before. A letter from the third century CE instructs someone to “make my slave-girl be properly industrious” in the context of a textile production operation that also involved paid weavers and specific dye colors.84

Girls were rarely apprenticed – only one documented female apprenticeship contract survives from the region, involving an orphaned girl working off her deceased father’s debts.85 But the absence of contracts does not mean the absence of women. It may mean only that women learned weaving informally, within households, without the legal apparatus of notarized agreements and penalty clauses. The contracts documented a particular social arrangement – a boy sent to a master outside his family – and that arrangement was, in the documents that survive, a male one. But the looms themselves were not gendered. The thread did not care who threw the shuttle.

sections.title: XV. Heraclas, Son of Tryphon
sections.body_html:
There is one more document in the trail, and it is tantalizing.

In 91 CE – twenty-five years after Tryphon apprenticed Thoonis to Ptolemaeus – a man named Heraclas, son of Tryphon, appears in a slave manumission document. He is about 31 years old, of middle height, fair, with a long face and a scar above his right knee.86 He is paying 200 drachmas toward the ransom of slaves held by men named Achilleus and Sarapas.

Heraclas, son of Tryphon. The patronymic matches. The physical description – fair, long-faced – echoes Tryphon’s own. Two hundred drachmas was a significant sum, more than Tryphon paid for his loom, more than the penalty clause in either apprenticeship contract. If this Heraclas was Tryphon’s son – a brother of Thoonis, or perhaps a nephew, or a grandson – then the weaving family had done well. They had money. They could afford to ransom slaves. The trade that Tryphon practiced with failing eyes and borrowed scribes had, by the next generation, produced modest prosperity.87

We cannot be certain of the connection. Common names, coincidental matches, the papyrologist’s perpetual anxiety. But the constellation – Heraclas son of Tryphon, fair-skinned, long-faced, in Oxyrhynchus, a generation after the archive – is suggestive.

sections.title: XVI. The Weaver's Guild
sections.body_html:
Weavers did not face the world alone. Not entirely.

A document from the Fayum village of Euhemeria, dated to the reign of Tiberius (15-36 CE), preserves the structure of a weavers’ guild. Heracles, son of Petesouchos, is identified as “head of the weavers” – prostates. Aphrodisius, son of Asclepiades, is the secretary – grammateus. Together they act as sureties for five individual weavers facing a legal claim from a wool-worker named Paninoutis.88

The guild had officers. It had a membership list. It functioned as a mutual support organization, with leaders pledging their personal guarantee on behalf of individual members. The dispute with the wool-worker hints at tensions between branches of the textile trade – weavers on one side, the people who processed the raw material on the other.

In Oxyrhynchus itself, the evidence for organized collective action comes later, from that senate meeting of 271-272 CE, where “a petition of the city cloth-weavers” was read aloud – the weavers acting not as individuals but as a group, collectively demanding higher pay for their work on temple linen.89 By then, the trade had a voice. Whether it had one in Tryphon’s day – whether the weavers of Temgenouthis Square organized against the Apollophaneses of the world, the corrupt ex-collectors who ripped tunics from their backs – we do not know.

We know only that three separate weavers filed three separate petitions with the same strategus about the same problem. They did not file jointly. Each man stood alone before the bureaucracy and asked for help.

sections.title: XVII. What We Do Not Know
sections.body_html:
We do not know whether Thoonis became a good weaver. We do not know whether Ptolemaeus taught him well or cut corners, whether the year was enough or whether Thoonis needed more. We do not know whether Tryphon lived to see his son’s first independent commission, or whether his cataracts took his sight entirely before the contract was up.

We do not know whether Thonis, the orphan boy apprenticed to Heraclas a century later, completed his five years. We do not know whether his uncle Ischyrion was alive at the end to collect the final tunic, worth 32 drachmas, or whether the boy had to finish alone.

We do not know how the slave Apollophanes died, or where. We do not know what the women at the looms – the taxpaying women whose names are half-erased – thought about a system that trained boys by contract and left girls to learn by watching.

We do not know the name of the tunic that Apollophanes the ex-collector tore from Sarapion’s back, or whether Sarapion ever wove himself a new one.

What we have are the contracts. The receipts. The complaints. The death notices and the tax rolls. Dry documents, most of them, composed in the formulaic Greek of the provincial notary, preserved in the dry sand of Oxyrhynchus for two thousand years. And in those documents – in the penalty clauses and the daily-absence fines, in the graduated wage scales and the rising price of tunics, in the mutual obligations and the mutual failures – the lives of children who stood at other men’s looms and learned, thread by thread, how to make the world’s cloth.

Thoonis at Ptolemaeus’s loom. Thonis at Heraclas’s loom. Two boys, a century apart, entering the same trade by the same mechanism, their days regulated by the same kind of contract, their absences counted and their holidays numbered, their masters bound by the same phrase: kathos auton epistato – as thoroughly as he knows it himself.

The shuttle passes. The beater falls. The fabric grows.

notes.text: P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394), dated 18 September 66 CE (Year 13 of Nero). Thoonis is described as "not yet of age" (*oudepo onta ton eton*). The typical age of entry for apprentices in Roman Egypt was 12-14 years.
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391), dated 22 May 37 CE (Year 1 of Gaius Caesar). Saraeus's dowry of 72 drachmas total: 40 in cash, gold earrings at 20 drachmas, milk-white robe at 12 drachmas.

The connection between the families is documented in scholarship on the weaving families of Oxyrhynchus. See "Three Weavers of the First Century CE: Tryphon of Oxyrhynchus, Pausiris of Oxyrhynchus, and Paul of Tarsus" (2019).
P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394): Ptolemaeus agrees to teach Thoonis "the weaver's art in all its branches of which Ptolemaeus has knowledge."

The Tryphon archive spans documents from approximately 37 CE (P.Oxy. 2.267, corpus ID 43391) to at least 66 CE (P.Oxy. 2.275, corpus ID 43394), with related documents extending to 91 CE (P.Oxy. 4.722, corpus ID 43608).
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391): Tryphon identifies himself as "a Persian of the Epigone."

P.Oxy. 1.99 (corpus ID 43190), dated 4 September 55 CE (Year 2 of Nero). Physical description of Tryphon: "about 47 years old, of middle height, fair, with a long face and a slight squint, and having a scar on his right wrist."

P.Oxy. 1.39dupl (corpus ID 28354), dated 24 April 52 CE (Year 12 of Claudius). "Release from service was granted by Gn. Vergilius Capito, praefect of Upper and Lower Egypt, to Tryphon, son of Dionysius, weaver, suffering from cataract and shortness of sight, of the metropolis of Oxyrhynchus. Examination was made in Alexandria."
P.Oxy. 2.264 (corpus ID 43389), dated 8 August 54 CE (Year 14 of Claudius). The loom was purchased through "the bank of Sarapion, son of Lochus, near the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus."
P.Oxy. 2.264 (corpus ID 43389): "I, Heraclides, son of Dionysius, wrote for him as he was illiterate."
P.Oxy. 2.264 (corpus ID 43389): the loom measured "three weavers' cubits less two palms, and containing two rollers and two beams." The weaver's cubit was approximately 0.525 meters.
P.Oxy. 1.99 (corpus ID 43190): Tryphon purchased half a three-storied house "for the sum of 32 talents of copper."
Wage estimates based on documented laborer payments in Roman Egypt. See Scheidel, "Real Wages in Roman Egypt," *Princeton/Stanford Working Papers in Classics* (2008).

P.Oxy. 2.264 (corpus ID 43389): "I will guarantee to you the sale with every guarantee, under penalty of payment to you of the price which I have received from you increased by half its amount, and of the damages."
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391), dated 22 May 37 CE.
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391): the cash was deposited "through the bank of Sarapion, son of Kleandrus, near the Serapeum."
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391). The term "milk-white" suggests bleached linen of fine grade.

P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391): "I will repay to you the 72 drachmae of silver on the 30th of Phaophi in the coming second year of Gaius Caesar... without any delay. If I do not repay... I will forfeit to you the said sum with the addition of half its amount."
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391): the scribe Didymus son of Boethus wrote Saraeus's acknowledgment "in astonishingly badly spelled Greek," per the original editors' note.
Demetrous is mentioned in earlier documents of the Tryphon archive. Saraeus was Tryphon's second wife.

The hereditary character of the weaving trade is confirmed by the consistent identification of Tryphon as "son of Dionysius, son of Tryphon" across multiple documents (P.Oxy. 2.275, corpus ID 43394; P.Oxy. 1.99, corpus ID 43190).

P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394): "The boy is to be fed and clothed during the whole period by his father Tryphon... on condition of a monthly payment to himself by Ptolemaeus of 5 drachmae on account of victuals, and at the termination of the whole period of a payment of 12 drachmae on account of clothing."
P.Oxy. 1.39dupl (corpus ID 28354). The exemption was granted in 52 CE; the apprenticeship contract dates to 66 CE, fourteen years later.
The practice of apprenticing sons to masters outside the family is documented across multiple weaving families of Oxyrhynchus.
"Three Weavers of the First Century CE" (2019): Pausiris of Oxyrhynchus apprenticed at least three sons to other master weavers.

Across all documented apprenticeships in Roman Egypt, durations ranged from 1 to 5 years with an average of approximately 2.5 years. See "Apprenticeship as Social Institution in Roman Egypt" (CAMWS 2014).
P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394). The financial terms are unusual: the master paid the father, rather than the reverse.

P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394): "if there are any days on which the boy fails to attend, Tryphon shall produce him for an equivalent number of days after the period is over, or shall forfeit for each day 1 drachma of silver."
P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394): "The penalty for taking him away within the period shall be 100 drachmae, and an equal sum to the treasury."
P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394): "If Ptolemaeus fails to instruct the boy thoroughly he is to be liable to the same penalties."
P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394): Ptolemaeus signed himself; Tryphon's signature was written by another hand. See also P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391), where Leon signed for Tryphon.
P.Oxy. 1.99 (corpus ID 43190): the house was "situated by the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus in the southern part of the street called Temgenouthis to the west of the lane leading to Shepherds' Street."
C.Pap.Gr. 2.1.9 (corpus ID 42875), dated 21 February 61 CE (Year 7 of Nero): "My slave Apollophanes a weaver, registered in Temgenouthis Square, died during absence."
C.Pap.Gr. 2.1.9 (corpus ID 42875): the notice was addressed "To Philiscus, farmer of the tax on weaving."
P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401), ca. 50 CE: Sarapion identifies himself as "a weaver of the city of Oxyrhynchus, living in Gymnasium square quarter."
P.Oxy. 2.284 (corpus ID 44673), ca. 50 CE: Alexandrus identifies himself as "a weaver of Oxyrhynchus, living in the quarter of the square of Thoeris."
P.Oxy. 73.4953 (corpus ID 141219), after 48 CE: Dius identifies himself as being "of the weavers of the quarter of Poimenike."

O.Bodl. 2.1010 (corpus ID 51060), dated 22-21 BCE (Year 9 of Augustus): "Dachis, the agent of Phidon, to Patemnophis, linen-weaver, greetings. You have paid for the loom the third installment: 40 drachmas."
O.Bodl. 2.1011 (corpus ID 51061), dated 6 February 17 BCE: Petechonsis assessed 20 drachmas. O.Bodl. 2.1012 (corpus ID 51062), dated 7 February 17 BCE: Leukis assessed 2 drachmas.
The disparity between linen-weaver and general weaver tax rates remains debated. It may reflect different sub-categories of the trade, different production volumes, or different regulatory regimes.

O.Bodl. 2.1015 (corpus ID 51064), dated 30 April 49 CE: "Petemenophis son of Thoteus has paid for the linen-weavers (tax) of year 9: four drachmas, total 4 drachmas, and the surcharges of 1 obol 1/2 obol."

APF 56.270.12 (corpus ID 50833), dated 21 February 167 CE: "Horos and associates, tax-farmers of weavers, to Porieuths son of Psenouphis. We have received from you the tax for Tybi and Mecheir, soiled drachmai eight, total 8 drachmai."
O.Bodl. 2.1016 (corpus ID 51065), dated 9 November 90 CE: "Horos son of Phaeris has paid for the weavers' tax of year 10: four drachmas."
O.Ashm. 43 (corpus ID 50880), dated 1 April 191 CE: "Premaos and associates, supervisors of the weavers' tax, to Pachnoumis son of Pakoibios."
O.Bodl. 2.1033 (corpus ID 51073), dated 197 CE: tax collected "in the name of Besarion and brothers."
O.Bodl. 2.1017 (corpus ID 51067), dated 141 CE: weaver's tax paid by "Ch[...]os daughter of Senpetemenophis."
O.Bodl. 2.1028 (corpus ID 51071), dated 188 CE: weaver's tax paid by "a slave-woman."
P.Oxy. 12.1438 (corpus ID 42498), late 2nd century CE: records of persons "not traced" who had fled to "unknown places" to avoid the cloth-weavers' tax.
P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401), ca. 50 CE: "Apollophanes, ex-collector of the trade tax upon weavers... using great violence seized from me a linen tunic which I was wearing, worth 8 drachmae."
P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401): Apollophanes "extorted from me four more drachmae, and two drachmae each month during the six months... total, 24 drachmae."
P.Oxy. 2.284 (corpus ID 44673), ca. 50 CE: "Apollophanes, ex-collector of taxes... extorted from me among other people 16 drachmae of silver."
P.Oxy. 73.4953 (corpus ID 141219), after 48 CE: "Ammonius, the ex-praktor, extorted from me in Year 6... 40 drachmas of silver, and in Year 7, another 40 drachmas of silver, bit by bit."
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611), dated 23 September 183 CE (Year 24 of Commodus).
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): "Ischyrion on the one part that he has apprenticed to Heraclas... Thonis, a minor." Ischyrion is identified as the deceased father's brother.
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): Heraclas is identified as "son of Sarapion also called Leon, son of Heraclides... weaver."
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): "I, Thonis also called Morous, son of Harthonis, wrote for him as he was illiterate."

P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): the apprentice was to attend "every day from sunrise to sunset, performing all the orders that may be given to him by the said teacher on the same terms as the other apprentices."
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): "For the first 2 years and 7 months of the 3rd year Heraclas shall pay nothing for the boy's wages."
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): graduated wages of 12 drachmas/month (remaining 5 months of year 3), 16 drachmas/month (year 4), and 24 drachmas/month (year 5).
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): annual tunic values of 16, 20, 24, 28, and 32 drachmas across the five-year term.
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): "The boy shall have 20 holidays in the year on account of festivals without any deduction from his wages."
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): days missed beyond the 20 holidays, "from idleness or ill-health or disobedience or any other reason," must be made up without wages.
P.Oxy. 4.725 (corpus ID 43611): Heraclas "agrees to instruct the apprentice in the aforesaid art within the period of 5 years as thoroughly as he knows it himself."
C.Pap.Gr. 2.1.9 (corpus ID 42875), dated 21 February 61 CE.
The requirement to notify the tax farmer of a weaver's death shows the degree to which the state tracked individual practitioners by name and location.
"Slave and Free Children in the Apprenticeship Contracts from Roman Egypt," identifying 11 of 39 documented apprentices as slaves.
P.Oxy. 12.1414 (corpus ID 43232), dated 271-272 CE.
P.Oxy. 12.1414 (corpus ID 43232): "the price of weaving-yarn is 49 denarii, and 11 denarii were paid to them from the Treasury's account."
P.Oxy. 12.1414 (corpus ID 43232): "Let the yarn-merchants be content with 19 denarii besides what was paid to them from the Treasury's account."
P.Oxy. 12.1414 (corpus ID 43232): the cloth-weavers petitioned for more money "on account of the rise in the value of the materials and in the wages of their workmen."
P.Oxy. 12.1414 (corpus ID 43232): "Let more drachmae be given to the cloth-weavers, making 200 drachmae in all."
P.Oxy. 12.1414 (corpus ID 43232): "Let the cloth-weavers who are to weave the linen of the temple appoint someone to (test it?)."
P.Oxy. 66.4530 (corpus ID 137588), late 3rd century CE (reign of Diocletian and Maximian).
P.Oxy. 66.4530 (corpus ID 137588): the oath-taker "provided as my sureties Aurelii Ammonios son of El[...]os from Toou, and Sarapion."
ZPE 207.145 (corpus ID 44705), dated 117-138 CE: a will bequeathing "a weaving rod measuring five cubits."
P.Oxy. 4.736 (corpus ID 43618), ca. 1 CE: household expense account listing "for the weaver's breakfast 1 ob.; beer for the weaver 1 ob." and "cost of weaving a cloak 1 dr. 2 ob."
On the shift from linen to wool in Roman Egypt, see P.Ryl. 2.189 (corpus ID 47953), where the weavers of Socnopaei Nesus provided 19 tunics and 5 white cloaks for soldiers serving in Judaea.
P.Oxy. 1.113 (corpus ID 43151), 2nd century CE: "I enclosed in the former packet a pattern of white-violet colour... for the tunic is to be woven immediately."
P.Oxy. 4.736 (corpus ID 43618): "cost of weaving a cloak 1 dr. 2 ob."
P.Hamb. 4.276 (corpus ID 50327), late 2nd-early 3rd century CE: "Theoninos, linen-weaver, 1 1/2 arouras." P.Stras. 9.897 (corpus ID 50582), 3rd century CE: "Perseus, weaver: arourai 3 1/2."
P.Oxy. 10.1269 (corpus ID 43199), early 2nd century CE: "a half share in three weavers' looms which belonged to his father."
O.Bodl. 2.1017 (corpus ID 51067), dated 141 CE.
O.Bodl. 2.1028 (corpus ID 51071), dated 188 CE.
P.Oxy. 7.1069 (corpus ID 44061), 3rd century CE: "Make my slave-girl be properly industrious."
"Slave and Free Children in the Apprenticeship Contracts from Roman Egypt." Only one documented female apprenticeship contract survives from the region.

P.Oxy. 4.722 (corpus ID 43608), dated 29 August 91 CE (Year 10 of Domitian): "Heraclas son of Tryphon... aged about 31, of middle height, fair, having a long face and a scar above his right knee, namely 200 drachmae of Imperial silver coin."
The 200 drachmas Heraclas contributed to the slave ransom was ten times the price Tryphon paid for his loom.
P.Ryl. 2.94 (corpus ID 35382), dated 15-36 CE (reign of Tiberius): "Heracles son of Petesouchos, head of the weavers of Euhemeria, and Aphrodisius son of Asclepiades, secretary of the same weavers."
P.Oxy. 12.1414 (corpus ID 43232): "A petition of the city cloth-weavers having been read."

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              Ammonius, son of Ammonius, to Tryphon, son of Dionysius, greeting. I agree that I have sold to you the weaver’s loom belonging to me, measuring three weavers’ cubits less two palms, and containing two rollers and two beams, and I acknowledge the receipt from you through the bank of Sarapion, son of Lochus, near the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus, of the price of it agreed upon between us, namely 20 silver drachmae of the Imperial and Ptolemaic coinage; and that I will guarantee to you the sale with every guarantee, under penalty of payment to you of the price which I have received from you increased by half its amount, and of the damages. This note of hand is valid. The 14th year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, the 15th of the month Caesareus.
              I, Ammonius, son of Ammonius, have sold the loom, and have received the price of 20 drachmae of silver and will guarantee the sale as aforesaid. I, Heraclides, son of Dionysius, wrote for him as he was illiterate.
              Date, and banker’s signature.

sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 Copy. The second year of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, on the 6th of the month Audnaeus=Sebastus, at Oxyrhynchus in the Thebaid, before the agoranomi Andromachus and Diogenes. Tryphon, son of Dionysius, about ... years old, of middle height, fair, with a long face and a slight squint, and having a scar on his right wrist, has bought from his mother Thamounis’ cousin, Pnepheros, son of Papontos, also an inhabitant of Oxyrhynchus, about 65 years old, of middle height, fair, having a long face and a scar above his ... eyebrow and another on his right knee, (the document being drawn up in the street) one half of a three-storied house inherited from his mother, together with all its entrances and exits and appurtenances, situated by the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus in the southern part of the street called Temgenouthis to the west of the lane leading to "Shepherds’ Street," its boundaries being, on the south and east, public roads, on the north, the house of the aforesaid Thamounis, mother of Tryphon the buyer, on the west, the house of Tausiris, sister of Pnepheros the seller, separated by a blind alley, for the sum of 32 talents of copper; and Pnepheros undertakes to guarantee the half share which is sold perpetually in every respect with every guarantee.

sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 Copy of a release dated and signed in the twelfth year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, Pharmouthi 29. Release from service was granted by Gn. Vergilius Capito, praefect of Upper and Lower Egypt, to Tryphon, son of Dionysius, weaver, suffering from cataract and shortness of sight, of the metropolis of Oxyrhynchus. Examination was made in Alexandria.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              Tryphon, son of Dionysius, a Persian of the Epigone, to Saraeus, daughter of Apion, under the wardship of Onnophris, son of Antipater, greeting. I acknowledge the receipt from you at the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus through the bank of Sarapion, son of Kleandrus, of 40 silver drachmae of the Imperial and Ptolemaic coinage, and for the value of one pair of gold earrings, 20 drachmae of silver, and for a milk-white robe, 12 drachmae of silver, making a total sum of 72 drachmae of silver, to which nothing at all has been added, in consideration of which I have consented (to our marriage). And I will repay to you the
              72 drachmae of silver on the 30th of Phaophi in the coming second year of Gaius Caesar Germanicus Novus Augustus Imperator without any delay. If I do not repay in accordance with the above terms I will forfeit to you the said sum with the addition of half its amount, for which you are to have the right of execution upon me and upon all my property, as in accordance with a legal decision. If we separate from each other, you shall be empowered to have the pair of earrings at their present value. And since we are living together without a marriage contract, I further agree if as aforesaid owing to a quarrel we separate from each other while you are in a state of pregnancy, to ... so long as you ... This receipt is valid wherever and by whomsoever it is produced.
              There follow (1) the signature of Tryphon, written for him by Leon, (2) the signature of Onnophris, the guardian of Saraeus, written on his behalf by Theon, son of Paaeis, (3) the docket of the bank through which the payment was made, (4) the signature of Saraeus, written for her, in astonishingly badly spelled Greek, by Didymus, son of Boëthus, acknowledging that she had received back the sum mentioned in the agreement. This acknowledgement of Saraeus is dated Payni 15 in the 3rd year of Claudius.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              Agreement between Tryphon, son of Dionysius, son of Tryphon, his mother being Thamounis, daughter of Onnophris, and Ptolemaeus, weaver, son of Pausirion, son of Ptolemaeus, his mother being Ophelous, daughter of Theon, both parties being inhabitants of the city of Oxyrhynchus. Tryphon agrees that he has apprenticed to Ptolemaeus his son Thoënis, whose mother is Saraeus, daughter of Apion, and who is not yet of age, for a term of one year from this day, to serve and to perform all the orders given him by Ptolemaeus in respect of his weaver’s art in all its branches of which Ptolemaeus has knowledge. The boy is to be fed and clothed during the whole period by his father Tryphon, who is also to be responsible for all the taxes upon him, on condition of a monthly payment to himself by Ptolemaeus of 5 drachmae on account of victuals, and at the termination of the whole period of a payment of 12 drachmae on account of clothing. Tryphon is not to have the power of taking away his son from Ptolemaeus until the completion of the period; and if there are any days on which the boy fails to attend, Tryphon shall produce him for an equivalent number of days after the period is over, or shall forfeit for each day 1 drachma of silver. The penalty for taking him away within the period shall be 100 drachmae, and an equal sum to the treasury. If Ptolemaeus fails to instruct the boy thoroughly he is to be liable to the same penalties. This contract of apprenticeship is valid.
              Date, and signature of Ptolemaeus.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 4
              The 10th year of the Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, on the 6th intercalary day of Hyperberetaeus, dies Augustus, which is the 6th intercalary day of the month Caesarius, dies Augustus, at Oxyrhynchus in the Thebaid, before three agoranomi called Psammis, Achilleus, aged about 20 years, of middle height, fair, having a long face and a scar on the middle of his forehead, and Sarapas, aged about ... years, of middle height, fair, having a long face and a scar on his left ..., both sons of ... son of Ammonius, their mother being Sarapous daughter of ..., all of Oxyrhynchus, have set free under sanction of Zeus, Earth, and Sun (the deed being drawn up in the street) the third part which they jointly own of the slave who has been freed as regards the other two-thirds, Apollonous, aged about 26, of middle height, fair, having a long face and a scar on the right foot, ... for ... drachmae 4 obols of coined silver and the ransom paid to Achilleus and Sarapas by Heraclas son of Tryphon son of ..., his mother being Taonnophris daughter of ... of the said city, aged about 31, of middle height, fair, having a long face and a scar above his right knee, namely 200 drachmae of Imperial silver coin and ... talents 1000 drachmae of copper; Achilleus or any one else on his behalf being forbidden to make any demand of the aforesaid ransom from Apollonous or her assigns, or to ... The certifier of the manumission is ... son of Peteésis, his mother being ..., of the said city, aged about 40, of middle height, fair, having a long face and a sear upon his ... shin, in the same street.
              I, Achilleus, have with my brother Sarapas effected the emancipation of the third part of the slave Apollonous, and I have received the ransom, two hundred drachmae of silver...

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 4
              Ischyrion son of Heradion and ..., of Oxyrhynchus, and Heraclas son of Sarapion also called Leon, son of Heraclides, his mother being ..., of the said city, weaver, agree with each other as follows :— Ischyrion on the one part that he has apprenticed to Heraclas ... Thonis, a minor, to be taught the art of weaving for a period of five years starting from the 1st of next month, Phaophi, and will produce him to attend the teacher, for the stipulated period every day from sunrise to sunset, performing all the orders that may be given to him by the said teacher on the same terms as the other apprentices, and being fed by Ischyrion. For the first 2 years and 7 months of the 3rd year Heraclas shall pay nothing for the boy’s wages, but in the remaining 5 months of the said 3rd year Heraclas shall pay for the wages of the said apprentice 12 drachmae a month, and in the 4th year likewise for wages 16 drachmae a month, and in the sth year likewise 24 drachmae a month; and Heraclas shall furnish for the said apprentice in the present 24th year a tunic worth 16 drachmae, and in the coming 25th year a second tunic worth 20 drachmae, and likewise in the 26th year another tunic worth 24 drachmae, and in the 27th year another tunic worth 28 drachmae, and likewise in the 28th year another tunic worth 32 drachmae. The boy shall have 20 holidays in the year on account of festivals without any deduction from his wages after the payment of wages begins; but if he exceeds this number of days from idleness or ill-health or disobedience or any other reason, Ischyrion must produce him for the teacher during an equivalent number of days, during which he shall remain and perform all his duties, as aforesaid, without wages, being fed by the said Ischyrion, because the contract has been made on these terms. Heraclas on the other part consents to all these provisions, and agrees to instruct the apprentice in the aforesaid art within the period of 5 years as thoroughly as he knows it himself, and to pay the monthly wages as above, beginning with the 8th month of the 3rd year. Neither party is permitted to violate any of the aforesaid provisions, the penalty for such violation being a fine of 100 drachmae to the party abiding by the contract and to the Treasury an equal sum. This agreement is valid. The 24th year of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Sarmaticus Germanicus Maximus, Thoth 25. I, Heraclas son of Sarapion also called Leon, have made this contract and consent to all the aforesaid provisions. I, Thonis also called Morous, son of Harthonis, wrote for him as he was illiterate.

Φιλίσκος σεσημ είωμαι . ἔτους ζ Νέρωνος Κλαυδίου Κα ίσαρος Σεβαστοῦ Γερ μανικοῦ Αὐτο κράτορ ος Με χ εὶρ κ ζ Σεβαστῇ.
sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Philiscus, farmer of the tax on weaving, from Sarapion, son of Sarapion. My slave Apollophanes a weaver, registered in Temgenouthis Square, died during absence in the present 7th year of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator. Wherefore I request that his name be inscribed in the list of dead persons, and I swear by Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator that this information is true.
              Date, and official signature of Philiscus.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Tiberius Claudius Pasion, strategus, from Sarapion, son of Theon, a weaver of the city of Oxyrhynchus, living in Gymnasium square quarter. Apollophanes, ex-collector of the trade tax upon weavers, in the first year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator using great violence seized from me a linen tunic which I was wearing, worth 8 drachmae. He also extorted from me four more drachmae, and two drachmae each month during the six months from the month Neos Sebastos in the ninth year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator to Pharmuthi; total, 24 drachmae. I therefore beg you to proceed against him as you may think fit.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Tiberius Claudius Pasion, strategus, from Alexandrus, son of Apollonius, a weaver of Oxyrhynchus, living in the quarter of the square of Thoëris. Apollophanes, ex-collector of taxes, in the eighth year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator extorted from me among other people 16 drachmae of silver. I therefore beg you to proceed against him as you may think fit.

sources.translation:
To Tiberius Claudius Pasion, strategus, from Dius son of Peteuris, of those from the city of the Oxyrhynchi, of 
the weavers of the quarter of Poimenike. Ammonius, the ex-praktor, extorted from me in Year 6 of Tiberius Claudius Caesar
Augustus Germanicus Imperator 40 drachmas of silver, and in Year 7,
 another 40 drachmas of silver, bit by bit. As a consequence, because of the extortion, I am in arrears for the trade tax for Year 7. I therefore request
 that you deal with (this) as you may see fit. Farewell. (J. Whitehorne)

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 10
              To ... from Diemous daughter of Colluthus, of the city of Oxyrhynchus], with her euardian Sarapion son of ..., of the said city .... I shut up the door of my house and the door of the terrace, and on my return I found that a box which I had in the terrace had been unfastened and that there had been abstracted from it two gold bracelets of the weight of four minae, a gold figure of Bes, and two large silver bracelets, and that the door of the terrace had been lifted. As I have some suspicion against my neighbours Heras son of Kalathus, weaver, and those working with him, because my house is easy of access from the house of Heras, I present this petition and request that, if you think fit, you should come for a personal inspection, and that Heras and his associates, whose names he will himself give, should be brought before you and the proper inquiry made, in order that I may be able with your assistance to discover my property. Date and signature of Diemous written for her by Sarapion.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 12
              (The prytanis said,) “... is owing, and 14 talents. The value of the 100 (?) garments is 14[0] talents, ... I have received the 63 belonging to the nome on account of the city’s share. Let them be compelled to pay monthly from their own property 74 (?)...”
              The prytanis said, “You examined the list of the temple and fixed a limit, and your resolution was submitted to the strategus, but (the priests?) ... made the excuse that those who undertook the work in question and their wives were unable to spin the yarn, ... (see) that on this point also you fix a limit, for there are only a few villages in your nome which have this material.” The senators said, ... Septimius Diogenes also called Agathodaemon, ex-hypomnematographus and however he is styled, syndic, said, “(The yarn-merchants report that) ... and the price of weaving-yarn is 49 denarii, and 11 denarii were paid to them from the Treasury’s account...” The senators said, “Let the yarn-merchants be content with 19 denarii besides what was paid to them from the Treasury's account.” Septimius Diogenes also called Agathodaemon, ex-hypomnematographus and however he is styled, syndic, said, “ If this is your decision in the case of the weaving-yarn, we will supply a sample and ... to those who are to weave it ... Let the cloth-weavers who are to weave the linen of the temple appoint (?) someone to (test it?).”
              A petition of the city cloth-weavers having been read, to the effect that, besides the ... drachmae paid to them in the past year (for each ...), .. more drachmae should be given to them on account of the rise in the value of the materials and in the wages of their workmen, after the reading the prytanis said, “Let .. more drachmae be given to the cloth-weavers, making 200 drachmae in all, on account of the rise in value of the materials. Decree this... When the bundle (?) has been valued, let these (drachmae) too accrue. Those who pay the value of the (cloth) ... it shall be submitted to you at the next meeting of the senate.” The senators said, “So be it.”
              (Date?) A communication from Terentius Arius, strategus, having been read, concerning the election of ..., it was decided to postpone the matter until the next meeting.
              A communication from the strategus having been read, concerning the election of other convoyers of animals, after the reading the prytanis said, “... especially the convoyers of the animals transported ... I collected some senators who were present and nominated one, Sarapion ... in order that there should not be (any delay)...” The senators said, ‘Invaluable prytanis ; save yourself for us, prytanis; excellent is your rule; excellent...” The prytanis said, “... is in the counting-house.” The senators said, “The prytanis has done right.”
              The prytanis said, “The law commands that the coming prytanis should be nominated six months beforehand. I remind you...” The senators said, “The nomination is made with a resolution...” The prytanis said, “... for I have (long) been ill and have a cough from my lung, as you know, ...” The senators said, “(Illustrious) prytanis, noble prytanis, still labour for us; labour in a manner worthy of the past.”

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 12
              Copy. ...s Nicon to the exactor of the Oxyrhynchite nome, greeting. It has come to my knowledge that Ptolemaeus, the collector of linen, has gone to Alexandria. So to prevent any deception from occurring in connexion with the completion (?) of the weaving of the said linen, take care to provide Sarapion the centurion with the assistance assigned to your division, and with the services of Antonius the officialis, in order that the persons responsible may be compelled to manufacture the clothing in irreproachable (?) materials, until the aforesaid Ptolemaeus comes. For this reason I have sent the aforesaid officialis. I pray for your health.

Translation: from P.Oxy. 4
              The 21st: ... through Zm... for the cloak of Coraxus, 10 drachmae; turnips for pickling 1 dr. 2 obols; for the kettle, payment for enamelling 2 ob.; salt 1 ob.; cost of grinding 1 artaba of wheat on the 18th 3 ob.; omelette for the bread 2 ob.; cost of mending the cloak of Coraxus 1 1/2 ob.; for treating (?) the wife of Gemellus 4 ob.; perfume for the dispatch of the mummy of the daughter of Phna 4 ob. The 22nd: a chous of oil 4 dr. 4 ob.; wax and stilus for the children 1 ob.; pure bread for Prima 4 ob.; for treating Tyche 3 ob. 9th Mecheir ... the 10th: ... for the weaver’s breakfast 1 ob.; ... for the Sarapeum 2 ob.; pure bread for the children 1/2 ob.; beer for the weaver 1 ob.; leeks for the weaver’s breakfast 1 ob.; a pigeon 1 ob.; to Antas 2 dr. 2 ob.; up at the city for the bread, cost of grinding 2 artabae of wheat, through Isas, 1 dr. 2 ob. The 11th: at the camp, through Theodorus, for the bread, cost of grinding 1 artaba of wheat 4 ob.; for the weaver’s breakfast 1 ob.; asparagus for the dinner of Antas when (he went) to the funeral feast of Athe... the fuller 1/2 ob.; and to the slaves (?), for a cabbage for dinner 1/2 ob.; to the child 1/2 ob.; ...The 16th: a relish 1/2 ob.; omelettes for the bread 2 1/2 ob. The 17th: milk for the children 1/2 ob.; pure bread 1/2 ob. The 18th: to Secundas, a cake for the children 1/2 ob. The 19th: barley water for the same 1/2 ob. The 20th: sauce 1 ob.; pure bread 1/2 ob.; for treating Antonia 2 ob.; and for Taptollous daughter of Caecilius 3 ob.; on the birthday of Tryphas, for garlands 2 ob.; on the birthday of ... for garlands 2 ob. The 21st: pomegranates for the children 1 ob.; playthings and ... for the children 1 ob.; beer 3 ob.; sauce 1 ob. The 22nd: sauce 1 ob.; Thaësis ... for 2 days 5 ob.; the mother of Ammonas for . days ...; Taarpaësis for 2 days 5 ob.; Berous similarly for 10 days 4 dr. 1 ob. The 24th: cost of grinding 1 artaba of wheat 4 ob.; 2 ... of pickle 2 ob.; salt 1 ob.; a needle and thread 1 ob.; cost of grinding 1 artaba of wheat, through Theodorus, 4 ob.; cost of weaving a cloak 1 dr. 2 ob.; pure bread for Ph... 1 dr.; a pigeon for the children 1 ob.; pure bread for the same 1/2 ob.; to Secundus for a cake for the children 1/2 ob., and for dry meal 1/2 ob.; milk 1/2 ob.; perfume for the mummy of the daughter of Pasis 1 dr.... The 10th: ... for the women 2 dr. 3 ob.; relishes for the women on 2 days 2 1/2 ob.; cost of tinkering a lamp 2 1/2 ob.; pulse when ... was dining here 1 1/2 ob.; for treating Laodice 2 1/2 ob.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 10
              To Ptolemaeus, priest and exegetes in office, from Theonas son of Isidorus and Soëris of the city of Oxyrhynchus, Appended is a copy of the list which I presented to Sarapion, your predecessor as exegetes, of the articles left to my nephew and niece, being minors, Sarapas and Sarapous, by their deceased father, who was my full brother, Isas.
              To Sarapion, priest and exegetes in office, from Theonas son of Isidorus and Soërous of the city of Oxyrhynchus. Being asked by you for a list of the furniture and other articles left to my nephew and niece, being minors, Sarapas and Sarapous, by their deceased father who was my full brother Isas, I declare that they are as follows: a coffer supplied with a false key, another out of use, a box of bronze, a plate or dish pledged by Isas during his lifetime to Panares for twenty drachmae of silver, another box likewise pledged for ten drachmae to the same Panares, a flask of tin pledged to the same person for four drachmae, a ... coloured ... pledged to me for a further sum of twelve drachmae, a tunic pledged to Tnephersoïs for eight drachmae, and a half share in three weavers’ looms which belonged to his father, a pig sold by me, Theon, after the death of my brother Isas for forty drachmae, a large chest of bronze, one cup of tin and one of silver, a pillow ...

sources.translation:
Year x of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian August in the month of Kaisarios, the 4th intercalary (?) day, at the city of Oxyrhynchos in the Thebaid, for good fortune.
                      Ploution, son of Ischyrion, son of Petesiris, his mother being Heras, daughter of Didas, from the city of Oxyrhynchos, has made this will, being sane and in his right mind, in the street.
                So long as I live I am to have full power over my own belongings to command in the way I wish, unhindered, so that the new provisions will remain valid […].
                      If I die with this (will), I bequeath to each of my sons - Ischyras also called Achillas being an adult, and Heras the elder (?) being an adult […] and to the
                      infant Ploution, all three born of Sarapous, daughter of Petronios, - if each lives, and if not, to his family, jointly and in equal shares […] and
                      […] part of the house in which there is a court and yard and other equipment and anything else which I will bequeath in any way whatsoever,  but except of
                      […] big wooden […], a banquet of acacia-wood with a pallet and a weaving rod measuring five cubits […]
                      and a four-footed seat and wooden kourikos: these shall be separately of the aforementioned Ploution […];
                      (when Ploution) comes of age Ischyras also called Achillas and Heras and Ploution (shall give?) to my wife and their mother, Sarapous, daughter of Petronios, […]
                      sixty-six drachmae which I shall appoint for her.  If a wet nurse (?) of my sons, Herakl[…]
                      […] from Paeimis, as long as she lives the right of dwelling without rent in the house in which I live, being in […].
                      No one else is allowed to transgress any of the aforementioned, and a transgressor is to forfeit […]
                      for each attempt (against this will) both the damage and a fine of three hundred drachmae of silver, and an equal sum to the Treasury, and the foregoing provisions shall nonetheless remain valid.
                      I bequeath nothing of my belongings to anyone else.  This will is valid, (hand 2) I, Ploution, son of Ischyrion, have made this will and
                      after my death I bequeath all my (belongings) to my sons - Ischyras also called Achillas, and Heras […] and Ploution
                      - in equal shares, except for the aforementioned banquet of
                      acacia-wood with a pallet and the weaving rod and seat: these shall be of Ploution […].
              When Ploution comes of age, the three sons (shall give?) […]
                      (to their mother) sixty-six drachmae. If the wet nurse of the sons, Herakl[…]
                      […] (lives), the right of dwelling, as above. I am 61 years old, with a scar on my left wrist and my seal represents […].
              (hand 3) I, Diogenes, son of Neon, son of Diogenes, from the same city, witness the will of Ploution
                      and I am thirty-eight years old, with a scar on my right leg and my seal represents E[…]. (hand 4) I, NN, son of NN,
                      son of NN, from the same city, witness the will of Ploution and
                      I am fifty-two years old, with a scar in the middle of my forehead and my seal represents […].
              (hand 5) I, […]kos, son of Horos, son of Horos, from the same city, witness the will of Ploution
                      and I am xy years old, with a scar on my left […] and my seal represents Dio[…]. (hand 6) I, NN, son of NN,
                      son of NN, from the same city, witness the will of Ploution and I am xy years old, without scars (?) and my seal represents
              […]. (hand 7) I, Didas, son of Thonis, son of Didas, from the same city, witness
                      the will of Ploution and I am 45 years old, with a scar on my left knee and my seal represents
              […]. (hand 8) I, […]n, son of Heras, son of Apion, from the same city, witness to the will of Ploution and I am xy years old,
                      with a scar on […] from the left side and my seal represent Harpokrates.
                      […] mnemoneion of the city of Oxyrhynchos […]
                      Translation: T. Derda – M. Nowak, ZPE 207 (2018) 148

sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 Corbolon to Heraclides, greeting. I send you the key by Horion and the piece of the lock by Onnophris, the camel-driver of Apollonius. I enclosed in the former packet a pattern of white-violet colour. I beg you to be good enough to match it and buy me two drachmas’ weight, and send it to me at once by any messenger you can find, for the tunic is to be woven immediately. I received everything you told me to expect by Onnophris safely. I send you by the same Onnophris six quarts of good apples. I thank all the gods to think that I came upon Plution in the Oxyrhynchite nome. Do not think that I took no trouble about the key. The reason is that the smith is a long way from us. I wonder that you did not see your way to let me have what I asked you to send by Corbolon, especially when I wanted it for a festival. I beg you to buy me a silver seal and to send it me with all speed. Take care that Onnophris buys me what Irene’s mother told him. I told him that Syntrophus said that nothing more should be given to Amarantus on my account. Let me know what you have given him that I may settle accounts with him. Otherwise I and my son will come for this purpose. I had the large cheeses from Corbolon. I did not however want large ones, but small. Let me know of anything that you want and I will gladly do it. Farewell. Payni the 1st. (PS.) Send me an obol’s worth of cake for my nephew.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 7
              Troïlus to his sister Maz, greeting. You will do well to have my white tunic made quickly in order that I may find it made if I come to you. I have written to Heraclides also so that you may receive my tools(?) and the wages of the weavers; and I wish to know how you are hurrying on the making of it. The purple is put with the tools. Be careful to send the letter to Thermouthion at Alexandria, for we may be able to load two camels with wheat for you and to send them to you. Make my slave-girl be properly industrious; and if Tamoun bear a child make her be assiduous with it. Be careful to have my tunic made properly, and let them put good measure into it, and be large-handed in the colouring. Buy a donkey for Nicetes in order that he may be able to accomplish your business; and reply to me if you want anything. I salute Heras. I pray for your health. P.S. I have written to Heraclides to give Tamoun 3,000 drachmae. (Addressed) From Troïlus to his sister Maz.

sources.translation: ‘Heracles son of Petesouchos, head of the weavers of Euhemeria, and Aphrodisius son of Asclepiades, secretary of the same weavers, to Heron agent of Sotas, exegetes, greeting. We acknowledge that we are sureties to you for Apheus son of Apheus, Harpagathes son of Orsenouphis, Heras son of Orsenouphis, Melas son of Hergeus, and Heracles son of Apollonius, all five weavers of the said village of Euhemeria, and that it is incumbent on us to produce them for you whenever you choose, to answer the claims stated in the petition of Paninoutis son of Aphrodisius, wool-worker. I the aforesaid Aphrodisius have written for Heracleus because he is illiterate. The ..th year of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, Epeiph 18.’

sources.translation: ‘We, Dionysius son of Socrates and the associate collectors of public clothing for the guards, have received from the weavers of the village of Socnopaei Nesus nineteen … tunics, total 19, and for the needs of the soldiers serving in Judaea five white cloaks, total 5.  The 13th year of the Emperor Caesar Trajanus Hadrianus Augustus, Choiak 22. (Signed) Received by me, Diogenes. Received also by me, Onesas. Received also by me, Philoxenus.’
